questions.question:
Explain the double entry system of bookkeeping.
(a) State the rule of double entry.
(b) Record the following transactions in the appropriate accounts:
   i. Started business with cash ₦500,000
   ii. Purchased goods on credit from Ade ₦80,000
   iii. Sold goods for cash ₦60,000
   iv. Paid rent ₦15,000 by cash
   v. Received ₦75,000 from debtor Bello
(c) State THREE advantages of the double entry system.
questions.answer:
(a) Rule of Double Entry:
Every business transaction affects at least two accounts. For every debit entry in one account, there must be an equal and corresponding credit entry in another account. The total debits must always equal the total credits.

Basic rules:
— Debit: Assets increase, Liabilities decrease, Expenses increase
— Credit: Assets decrease, Liabilities increase, Income increases

(b) Recording transactions:

i. Started business with cash ₦500,000:
Debit: Cash Account ₦500,000 (asset increases)
Credit: Capital Account ₦500,000 (owner's equity increases)

ii. Purchased goods on credit from Ade ₦80,000:
Debit: Purchases Account ₦80,000 (expense/stock increases)
Credit: Ade's Account (Creditor) ₦80,000 (liability increases)

iii. Sold goods for cash ₦60,000:
Debit: Cash Account ₦60,000 (asset increases)
Credit: Sales Account ₦60,000 (income increases)

iv. Paid rent ₦15,000 by cash:
Debit: Rent Account ₦15,000 (expense increases)
Credit: Cash Account ₦15,000 (asset decreases)

v. Received ₦75,000 from debtor Bello:
Debit: Cash Account ₦75,000 (asset increases)
Credit: Bello's Account (Debtor) ₦75,000 (asset decreases)

(c) Three advantages of double entry:
1. Accuracy check — A trial balance can be extracted to verify that debits equal credits, revealing arithmetic errors.
2. Complete record — Every transaction is fully recorded showing both the giving and receiving aspect.
3. Facilitates preparation of financial statements — The ledger provides all information needed for the trading account, profit and loss account, and balance sheet.
4. Fraud detection — The system makes it harder to conceal fraudulent transactions since every entry has a corresponding entry.

questions.question:
Explain depreciation in accounting.
(a) Define depreciation and state FOUR causes.
(b) A machine was purchased on 1 January 2022 for ₦400,000. It has an estimated useful life of 5 years and a residual value of ₦50,000.
   i. Calculate the annual depreciation using the straight-line method.
   ii. Show the depreciation for the first three years using the reducing balance method at 20% per annum.
(c) State TWO effects of depreciation on financial statements.
questions.answer:
(a) Depreciation is the systematic allocation of the cost of a tangible fixed asset over its estimated useful life. It represents the reduction in the value of an asset due to wear and use.

Four causes of depreciation:
1. Physical wear and tear — Continuous use of machinery and equipment causes deterioration.
2. Obsolescence — Assets become outdated due to technological advancement (e.g. old computers replaced by modern ones).
3. Passage of time — Some assets (e.g. leasehold property) lose value simply with time.
4. Depletion — Wasting assets such as mines and oil wells are used up over time.
5. Accidents and damage — Physical damage reduces the value and useful life of assets.

(b) i. Straight-Line Method:
Formula: Annual Depreciation = (Cost - Residual Value) / Useful Life
Annual Depreciation = (₦400,000 - ₦50,000) / 5
Annual Depreciation = ₦350,000 / 5 = ₦70,000 per year

ii. Reducing Balance Method at 20%:
Year 1:
Book value at start: ₦400,000
Depreciation (20%): ₦400,000 × 20% = ₦80,000
Book value at end: ₦400,000 - ₦80,000 = ₦320,000

Year 2:
Book value at start: ₦320,000
Depreciation (20%): ₦320,000 × 20% = ₦64,000
Book value at end: ₦320,000 - ₦64,000 = ₦256,000

Year 3:
Book value at start: ₦256,000
Depreciation (20%): ₦256,000 × 20% = ₦51,200
Book value at end: ₦256,000 - ₦51,200 = ₦204,800

(c) Two effects of depreciation on financial statements:
1. Profit and Loss Account — Depreciation is charged as an expense, reducing the net profit for the year.
2. Balance Sheet — The accumulated depreciation is deducted from the cost of the fixed asset, showing its net book value (carrying value) which decreases each year.

questions.question:
Prepare a Trading, Profit and Loss Account.
From the following information for the year ended 31 December 2023:
Sales: ₦850,000 | Opening stock: ₦120,000 | Purchases: ₦480,000 | Closing stock: ₦95,000 | Carriage inwards: ₦12,000 | Returns inwards: ₦20,000 | Returns outwards: ₦15,000 | Wages: ₦90,000 | Rent: ₦45,000 | Electricity: ₦18,000 | Depreciation: ₦30,000 | Discount allowed: ₦8,000

(a) Prepare the Trading Account to find Gross Profit.
(b) Prepare the Profit and Loss Account to find Net Profit.
(c) State TWO uses of the profit and loss account.
questions.answer:
(a) Trading Account for the year ended 31 December 2023:

Dr Side (Costs):                          Cr Side (Income):
Opening Stock:      ₦120,000             Sales:              ₦850,000
Purchases: ₦480,000                      Less Returns In:   (₦20,000)
Less Returns Out: (₦15,000)              Net Sales:          ₦830,000
Net Purchases:      ₦465,000
Carriage Inwards:   ₦12,000
Cost of Goods Available: ₦597,000
Less Closing Stock: (₦95,000)
Cost of Goods Sold: ₦502,000
Gross Profit (balancing figure): ₦328,000
Total:              ₦830,000             Total:              ₦830,000

Gross Profit = Net Sales - Cost of Goods Sold
Gross Profit = ₦830,000 - ₦502,000 = ₦328,000

(b) Profit and Loss Account for the year ended 31 December 2023:

Dr (Expenses):                            Cr (Income):
Wages:              ₦90,000              Gross Profit b/d:   ₦328,000
Rent:               ₦45,000
Electricity:        ₦18,000
Depreciation:       ₦30,000
Discount Allowed:   ₦8,000
Total Expenses:     ₦191,000
Net Profit:         ₦137,000
Total:              ₦328,000             Total:              ₦328,000

Net Profit = Gross Profit - Total Expenses
Net Profit = ₦328,000 - ₦191,000 = ₦137,000

(c) Two uses of the profit and loss account:
1. It shows the net profit or net loss made by the business during the accounting period, helping owners assess business performance.
2. It helps management identify areas of high expenditure that need to be controlled to improve profitability.
3. It is used by banks and investors to assess the financial health and creditworthiness of the business.

questions.question:
Explain bank reconciliation statement.
(a) Define bank reconciliation statement and state WHY it is prepared.
(b) From the following information, prepare a bank reconciliation statement as at 31 March 2024:
— Cash book (bank column) balance: ₦185,000 (Dr)
— Bank statement balance: ₦210,000
— Unpresented cheques: ₦42,000
— Outstanding lodgements: ₦17,000
(c) State THREE causes of differences between the cash book and bank statement.
questions.answer:
(a) A bank reconciliation statement is a document prepared periodically to explain and reconcile the differences between the balance shown in the business's cash book (bank column) and the balance shown on the bank statement received from the bank.

Why it is prepared:
1. To identify and correct errors in the cash book or bank statement.
2. To detect fraud or unauthorised transactions.
3. To identify timing differences (unpresented cheques, outstanding lodgements).
4. To ensure the cash book is up to date with bank charges, interest, and direct credits.

(b) Bank Reconciliation Statement as at 31 March 2024:

Balance as per Bank Statement:                    ₦210,000
Less: Unpresented cheques                        (₦42,000)
(Cheques issued but not yet presented to bank)
Add: Outstanding lodgements                       ₦17,000
(Deposits made but not yet credited by bank)
Balance as per Cash Book:                         ₦185,000 ✓

Verification:
₦210,000 - ₦42,000 + ₦17,000 = ₦185,000 ✓ (matches cash book balance)

(c) Three causes of differences:
1. Unpresented cheques — Cheques written and recorded in the cash book but not yet presented to the bank for payment by the recipient.
2. Outstanding lodgements (deposits in transit) — Deposits recorded in the cash book but not yet credited on the bank statement.
3. Bank charges — Charges deducted by the bank (e.g. commission, maintenance fees) appearing on the bank statement but not yet recorded in the cash book.
4. Direct credits — Payments received directly into the bank account (e.g. standing orders, NEFT transfers) not yet recorded in the cash book.
5. Errors — Mistakes made in the cash book or by the bank in recording transactions.

questions.question:
Discuss partnership accounts.
(a) Define a partnership and state the contents of a partnership agreement.
(b) The following information relates to Alpha and Beta partnership for the year ended 31 December 2024:
— Net profit before appropriation: ₦240,000
— Alpha's salary: ₦40,000 | Beta's salary: ₦30,000
— Interest on capital: Alpha ₦20,000, Beta ₦15,000
— Profit sharing ratio: Alpha 3 : Beta 2
Prepare the Profit Appropriation Account.
(c) State TWO advantages of partnership over sole proprietorship.
questions.answer:
(a) A partnership is a business organisation formed by two or more persons (maximum 20, or 50 for professional firms) who agree to carry on a business together with the aim of making profit, sharing resources, and bearing losses jointly.

Contents of a Partnership Agreement (Deed of Partnership):
1. Names and addresses of all partners.
2. Nature and name of the business.
3. Amount of capital contributed by each partner.
4. Profit and loss sharing ratio.
5. Salaries payable to partners (if any).
6. Interest on capital and interest on drawings rates.
7. Procedure for admitting new partners or retiring existing ones.
8. Procedure for dissolving the partnership.
9. Duties and responsibilities of each partner.

(b) Profit Appropriation Account for year ended 31 December 2024:

Dr Side:                                  Cr Side:
Partners' Salaries:                       Net Profit b/d: ₦240,000
  Alpha:          ₦40,000
  Beta:           ₦30,000
Total Salaries:   ₦70,000

Interest on Capital:
  Alpha:          ₦20,000
  Beta:           ₦15,000
Total Interest:   ₦35,000

Residual profit to share:
₦240,000 - ₦70,000 - ₦35,000 = ₦135,000

Share of residual profit (3:2):
  Alpha: 3/5 × ₦135,000 = ₦81,000
  Beta:  2/5 × ₦135,000 = ₦54,000

Summary — Total allocated to each partner:
Alpha: ₦40,000 + ₦20,000 + ₦81,000 = ₦141,000
Beta:  ₦30,000 + ₦15,000 + ₦54,000 = ₦99,000
Total: ₦141,000 + ₦99,000 = ₦240,000 ✓

(c) Two advantages of partnership over sole proprietorship:
1. More capital — Partners pool their resources, providing more capital for the business than a single owner could provide.
2. Shared expertise and responsibilities — Different partners bring different skills (e.g. one partner manages finances, another handles operations), improving business efficiency.
3. Shared risk — Losses and liabilities are shared among partners, reducing the burden on any single individual.
